(17:37 GMT) Samsara Lifts Outlook as Large Companies Seek to Digitize

By Connor Hart

Large companies are digitizing to succeed in today's landscape, and they are banking on Samsara to help them, Chief Executive Sanjit Biswas said.

"In many cases, these companies haven't changed the way they're run for decades," he said Friday. "Then every once in a while there's this big leap forward where they say, 'Wow, we can do this in a fundamentally better way.'"

"We're excited to be powering that."

The San Francisco company provides software to manage vehicle fleets, leveraging data analysis and artificial intelligence to help companies operate more efficiently. Several large contract wins during the recent quarter lifted revenue and prompted the company to raise its full-year outlook.

Shares jumped 15% to $41.07. Despite the gain, shares have slipped about 6.6% so far this year.

During its latest quarter, Samsara inked deals with companies including Alaska Airlines and SRM Concrete, which bills itself as the largest ready-mix concrete supplier in the U.S. With these wins, large customers are becoming a bigger part of Samsara's business, Biswas said. Contracts valued at more than $1 million now account for more than one-fifth of the company's annual recurring revenue.

Samsara continues to win contracts by solving practical problems and demonstrating quick returns on investment, Biswas said. He cited the company's partnership with Mohawk Industries, where the flooring company saved nearly $8 million by reconfiguring its delivery routes with Samsara's software.

"The system more than paid for itself right there," he said, adding that the partnership also improved driver safety and customer satisfaction by providing real-time ETAs.

Samsara's technology is also solving challenges related to maintenance and fuel efficiency. By analyzing sensor data with AI, the software can predict when equipment needs servicing, coach drivers to reduce fuel use and flag risky behaviors before they lead to accidents, Biswas said.

And that push isn't limited to the U.S.

The company is also expanding abroad, with Biswas highlighting strong growth across Europe.

Looking forward, Samsara now expects adjusted earnings per share of 45 cents to 47 cents on revenue of $1.57 billion to $1.58 billion in fiscal 2026. It had previously guided for adjusted earnings per share of 39 cents to 41 cents on revenue of $1.55 billion to $1.56 billion.

In the current quarter, the company forecast adjusted earnings of 11 cents a share to 12 cents a share and revenue of $398 million to $400 million. Analysts polled by FactSet were looking for adjusted earnings of 10 cents a share and $395 million in revenue.

For the fiscal second quarter, Samsara posted a loss of 3 cents a share, narrower than its loss of 9 cents a share a year earlier. Adjusted earnings were 12 cents a share, 5 cents higher than Wall Street's model.

Revenue surged 30% to $391.5 million, ahead of analyst estimates for $372.2 million.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

Unemployment Rising for Young, Old — WSJ
data.description:
By Oyin Adedoyin

Unemployment is rising for people at the very beginning and very end of their careers.

Joblessness for people ages 20-24 rose to 9.2% in August from 8% a year ago. Among those age 65 and older, the increase was to 4.2% from 3.3%. Overall, unemployment has been broadly steady over that period.

Being unemployed can have big financial repercussions. Older workers can struggle to land new jobs, forcing them to dip into their savings or pause saving for retirement entirely. Recent college graduates who can't find employment may struggle to gain a financial foothold.

Laura Ullrich, director of economic research at Indeed, knows firsthand what she calls a "a very weak year in terms of job growth." Her son graduated with a bachelor's degree in the spring and since has enrolled in graduate school.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: US stocks brush record highs as weak jobs data fuel rate cut bets
data.description:
Key points:
  • US stocks hit record highs before pulling back
  • US nonfarm payrolls up 22,000 last month vs forecast of 75,000 gain
  • Some investors now price higher chance of 50 bps rate cut by Fed

U.S. stocks briefly hit record highs on Friday before reversing course to trade lower, after data showing U.S. job growth cooled in August led investors to double down on bets that the Federal Reserve might cut interest rates later this month, maybe by as much as 50 basis points.

Speculation that the Fed might lower rates more aggressively caused Treasury yields to nosedive and knocked the U.S. dollar lower, but drove gold to a new record high that drew closer to $3,600 per ounce.

Lower interest rates, which could lead to lower borrowing costs for businesses, are generally seen to be a boon to equity markets. Gold, which does not pay interest, also tends to shine when rates are low and uncertainty is high.

"This number today puts a 50-basis-point rate cut at the next meeting back on the table," Art Hogan, a strategist at B Riley Wealth Management in Boston, said about the Fed's upcoming policy meeting on September 17. "More significantly, I think 75-basis-point before the end of the year is now pretty much of a lock."

U.S. data showed nonfarm payrolls increased by only 22,000 jobs last month after rising by an upwardly revised 79,000 in July, missing forecasts for a gain of 75,000 positions.

The S&P 500 Index hit a record high of 6,532.65 points in early trade before pulling back to be down 0.55%. The Dow Jones Industrial Average also hit a record high in the first few minutes of the trading day before retreating 0.6%, while the Nasdaq Composite Index lost 0.3%.

In line with expectations of lower rates, the two-year Treasury yield (US2YT=RR) was last down 10.5 basis points at 3.49%, while the benchmark 10-year yield sagged 10 basis points to 4.076%.

By 1710 GMT, the MSCI World Equity Index EURONEXT:IACWI was flat on the day while Europe's STOXX 600 was down 0.2%. The FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100 was unchanged and France's CAC 40 lost 0.3%.

The U.S. dollar eased, with the dollar index down 0.6% at 97.674 , while the euro was up 0.6% at $1.17625 .

"The warning bell that rang in the labor market a month ago just got louder," said Olu Sonola, head of U.S. economic research at Fitch Ratings in New York.

"A weaker-than-expected jobs report all but seals a 25-basis-point rate cut later this month."

Fed Chair Jerome Powell had already reinforced rate cut speculation with an unexpectedly dovish speech at last month's Fed symposium in Jackson Hole.

Market sentiment has recovered in recent days after global stocks fell earlier this week and long-date bond yields in Europe hit their highest in years, as investors became concerned about the state of various countries' finances, particularly Britain and France.

Yields eased on Friday, with France's 30-year yield at 4.3873%, down from a peak of 4.523% on Wednesday (FR30YR=RR), and the UK's 30-year yield at 5.553%, after borrowing costs hit their highest level since 1998 earlier in the week.

The benchmark 10-year German yield was at 2.7051% (DE10YT=RR). German industrial orders unexpectedly fell in July, data on Friday showed.

After months of negotiations, the U.S. signed a deal to impose lower auto tariffs on Japan. The dollar was down 0.9% against the yen, with the pair at 147.05 .

Oil prices were in their third day of declines. Brent crude futures fell 2.3% to $65.44 a barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude eased 2.6% to $61.83.

The European Union's energy commissioner said the bloc would welcome U.S. President Donald Trump's plan to stop buying Russian oil.

Spot gold was up 1.3% at $3,592.13 per ounce, having hit a record $3,597.66 earlier. The metal is now on track for its strongest weekly gain in nearly four months.

data.title: Corporates, MBS, TIPS Seen as Options for Investors Leaving Money Markets — Market Talk
data.description:
1340 ET - Investors sitting on money markets have plenty of options to move into longer-term assets to capture yield, CIBC's Timothy Musial says. "If you are looking at corporate bonds, despite very tight spreads…we think that there is a place for that in your portfolio," he says. He recommends mortgage-backed securities. "You can pick up a decent amount of yield, in some instances more than corporates, for a very high quality asset." Inflation-protected Treasurys, or TIPS, are an option for investors worried about the impact of tariffs on prices. Musial likes one- to 10-year bonds and would avoid the longer end. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1330 ET - The Fed looks ready to resume interest-rate cutting and longer-term Treasury yields are falling less than shorter-term ones. Would that be enough to finally trigger the big migration out of money market funds analysts have been predicting since last year? CIBC's Timothy Musial thinks it might. Money market funds have $7.26 trillion deposited, according to the Investment Company Institute. But returns are lower than a year ago, while other fixed income options are paying more. "You do need to move out the curve a little bit to capture the same amount of yield, or even more," he says. "You want to stay in very high quality credit." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1325 ET - August's surprisingly weak job creation, plus June's revision to a negative number, drove markets to start pricing a 50-basis point interest rate cut by the Fed this month. CIBC's Timothy Musial says investors are overreacting. Most bets, however, are on a sequence of 25-bp cuts. "I think that's a more likely outcome." Musial expects the Fed to keep its options open after the September 17 meeting and won't commit to an October cut. "You still have GDP growth estimated to be at 3%, you still have inflation above their target," he says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1317 ET - The latest weak Canadian employment data are in keeping with BofA Securities expectations the Bank of Canada will be compelled to resume cutting interest rates in two weeks. BofA argues the unemployment rate, which rose to 7.1% in August, is clearly above any measure of a natural rate for Canada while inflation is below the central bank's target and inflation expectations are anchored at 2%. As such, it expects the central bank's policy rate to move into expansionary territory with two more quarter percentage point cuts following in October and December to take it to 2% by year-end. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1302 ET - The biggest-ever seasonally unadjusted employment plunge in Canada for an August, and the roughly 106,000 jobs lost in two months, is bound to catch the Bank of Canada's attention, Scotiabank's Derek Holt says. It raises the odds the central bank will resume easing, and now has Scotiabank penciling in a quarter percentage point rate cut this month and another in October. "If they're going to ease, then it's totally pointless to do it just once," Holt reckons. Still, the economist adds that easing as an insurance policy now may be regretted later, since inflationary pressures haven't gone away, including supply chain turmoil, higher inventory holdings as a buffer, labor market wage growth and productivity pressures. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1214 ET - Secondhand shopping has surged this year with consumers turning to thrift stores as tariffs cause price hikes in conventional apparel. But thrift stores were gaining even before tariffs, according to a report from Placer.ai. Visits to thrift stores, which rose 7.9% year-over-year in the second quarter, have risen 39.5% from the second quarter of 2019, while the broader clothing industry has seen only 9.5% growth over that span. Economic pressures and sustainability concerns have fueled that growth, which has extended beyond thrift store's traditional base to include more visits from rural and suburban consumers and higher-income demographics, the report says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1206 ET - The latest data show the labor market in Canada is deteriorating, having lost about 100,000 jobs over the past two months. Alberta Central's Charles St-Arnaud said that while there are indications that overall economic activity is no longer deteriorating, it isn't rebounding strongly either and sectors affected by the U.S. tariffs are likely to continue to struggle. This means the labor market will likely remain weak and further job losses are likely in the coming months, he says. The economist says the greater amount of slack in the economy than expected raises the likelihood of an interest rate cut in September, but adds that may still hinge on the next inflation release due out the day before the Bank of Canada's Sept. 17 policy meeting. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1146 ET - The impact of tariffs and economic disruption is starting to show, and the numbers are worrying, says Anupriya Gangopadhyay, economist at the Canadian Chamber of Commerce. The unemployment rate rose in August to a nine-year high, outside the pandemic, after the economy lost 65,500 jobs. Gangopadhyay says it is troubling is that the pain isn't just in export-heavy sectors such as manufacturing and trade, but also in areas such as tech, professional services, and even education. And it is a weak job market for young Canadians. "The warning lights are flashing." (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1053 ET - Gold prices rise further in afternoon trade as the latest U.S. jobs data likely sealed the case for the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates at its next meeting in two weeks. Futures rise 0.7% to $3,632.70 a troy ounce and are on track for a weekly gain of 3.3%. The U.S. added 22,000 jobs in August, below the 75,000 gain economists had expected, figures from the Labor Department showed. "The gold price has now finally broken out of its months-long trading range to the upside," Commerzbank Research's Barbara Lambrecht says. The rally is also fueled by concerns over the independence of the Fed and heightened geopolitical risks supporting safe-haven demand. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1036 ET - The dollar extends losses, weighed down by August's disappointing job creation. The meager 22,000 increase in payrolls "signals a substantial slowdown in job growth, reinforcing concerns about economic weakness," MH Markets' Mohammed Taha writes. The data keep the door open for a Fed cut this month and fuels bets on a hefty 50-basis point trim. "Lower interest rate expectations reduce the appeal of USD-denominated assets, driving capital outflows and weakening the USD," Taha says. Treasury yields fall sharply on the data, which he says adds pressure on the greenback. The WSJ Dollar Index falls 0.6%, as the dollar weakens 1% versus the Swiss franc and the yen and 0.8% to the euro. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1029 ET - Tight credit spreads and strong performance in the European credit market have prompted issuance of asset-backed securities (ABS) in new jurisdictions such as Poland and Norway, S&P Global Ratings' Arnaud Checconi says in a report. Asset-backed securities are investments backed by income-producing assets such as loans and leases. "European ABS issuance volumes for 2025 are on track to exceed those in 2024," Checconi adds. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1019 ET - Many long-time homeowners are sitting on substantial equity gains, but today's shifting market means selling successfully requires more strategy and preparation than in years past, Realtor.com says. Starting the process early--before the peak spring season--can make the difference between a smooth sale and missed opportunities. Nearly half--45.2%--of homeowners have lived in their homes for more than 15 years, with one in four staying put for more than 25 years. U.S. home prices have tripled from $145,000 in June 2000 to $435,300 in June 2025, according to the National Association of Realtors. For many owners, that means hundreds of thousands of dollars in potential equity. A typical homeowner who bought in 2005 has seen their home value rise by about 90%, boosting equity by more than $200,000. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

    CORPORATION (TSXV: CH; OTCQB: CHHYF;FSE: K47) (the “Company” or “CHARBONE”),a company focused on green hydrogen production and distribution,is pleased to announce it has signed, onSeptember 4, 2025, an Asset Purchase Agreement to acquire operationalhydrogen production and refuelling equipment in Quebec. The strategicacquisition will enable CHARBONE to fast-track the commissioning ofCHARBONE’s flagship Sorel-Tracy facility phase 1 and empowerCHARBONE to produce and deliver first industrial high purity hydrogen(UHP) sales in the upcoming quarter.

    The equipment, currently in usewill be dismantled, repurposed and relocated toSorel-Tracy.

    This transaction follows CHARBONE’s signing of anon-dilutive USD 50 millionconstruction capital facility announced on May 1and June 4, 2025. While this facility is earmarked for broader projectfinancing rather than this equipment purchase, it demonstratesCHARBONE’s strengthened capital position and ability to scale up itsoverall development plan.

    Key Investor Highlights

    • Accelerated Timeline: Repurposing proven operating equipment reduces installationcosts of new equipment — enabling production by early Q4 2025

Would that be enough to finally trigger the big migration out of money market funds analysts have been predicting since last year? CIBC's Timothy Musial thinks it might. Money market funds have $7.26 trillion deposited, according to the Investment Company Institute. But returns are lower than a year ago, while other fixed income options are paying more. "You do need to move out the curve a little bit to capture the same amount of yield, or even more," he says. "You want to stay in very high quality credit." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1325 ET - August's surprisingly weak job creation, plus June's revision to a negative number, drove markets to start pricing a 50-basis point interest rate cut by the Fed this month. CIBC's Timothy Musial says investors are overreacting. Most bets, however, are on a sequence of 25-bp cuts. "I think that's a more likely outcome." Musial expects the Fed to keep its options open after the September 17 meeting and won't commit to an October cut. "You still have GDP growth estimated to be at 3%, you still have inflation above their target," he says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1314 ET - Lululemon CEO Calvin McDonald says its customers that spend the most are responding well to new styles, rather than some of its core franchises. "Our high-value guests are overindexing on our new styles, so they're engaged," he says during a CNBC interview, adding the company needs to bring more newness and innovation to those guests in particular. "I would say our mix doesn't represent what the guest is reacting to," he says. He says he's confident in the company's continued efforts to reinvigorate design, with plans for a higher share of new products in the overall mix next year. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1301 ET - Lululemon CEO Calvin McDonald says the company's full-year guidance cut is a result of higher tariff rates as well as the end of the so-called de minimus rule, which allowed packages valued at $800 or less to enter the U.S. tariff free. About two-thirds of Lululemon's online orders were shipped from its facility in Canada, and a majority were below the threshold, he says. "We are in the work of shifting and changing our distribution network as a result," he says in a CNBC interview. "But it will take some time to make those adjustments." The company is also looking to cut costs internally and raise the prices of some products, he says. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1258 ET - Consumers are still hungry for protein, even as they pull back on spending at restaurants and on some discretionary groceries, Tyson Foods Chief Operating Officer Devin Cole said during the Barclays Global Consumer Staples Conference. Animal protein in particular is seen as an essential part of people's diets, now more so than ever, Cole said. Chicken is especially popular because of its value relative to other proteins, he said. "We are seeing that value-added business grow, thankfully, even with traffic counts being down." Tyson is responding by adding seasoned and marinated options, as well as more convenient protein sources such as frozen. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1252 ET - Chipotle still offers compelling value to customers, but consumers seem to be turning away because of their perception that fast casual and quick service restaurants have poor value overall, according to BTIG's Peter Saleh and Ben Parente. The analysts say many meals across the industry are approaching $15, and that a BTIG pricing survey found Chipotle had a national entree average of $10.31. They say their pricing work indicates that Cava and Sweetgreen charge 30% to 40% more for similar entrees. "We believe there is an opportunity here for management to lean into messaging around the $10 price point, a competitive moat that peers have not matched and cannot match," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1229 ET - Docusign sees the government as an opportunity for growth, CEO Allan Thygesen says in an interview with CNBC. The electronic-signature company recently signed an agreement with the General Services Administration to give government agencies a discount on Docusign products, Thygesen says. Docusign's work with federal agencies has been relatively small, but it presents a large opportunity, he says. The company is also trying to push more AI tools beyond just signatures, which help summarize and analyze agreements, and is aiming for larger customers. Shares up 5.3%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1214 ET - Secondhand shopping has surged this year with consumers turning to thrift stores as tariffs cause price hikes in conventional apparel. But thrift stores were gaining even before tariffs, according to a report from Placer.ai. Visits to thrift stores, which rose 7.9% year-over-year in the second quarter, have risen 39.5% from the second quarter of 2019, while the broader clothing industry has seen only 9.5% growth over that span. Economic pressures and sustainability concerns have fueled that growth, which has extended beyond thrift store's traditional base to include more visits from rural and suburban consumers and higher-income demographics, the report says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1155 ET - Toll Brothers is dialing back some of its home starts to clear its accumulated inventory of spec houses in a dormant housing market, says Wedbush analyst Jay McCanless. Toll Brothers primarily builds higher-end, luxury homes and as a result may not be suffering from the competitive supply that is forcing builders to offer aggressive incentives in the entry-level homes market. Still, McCanless says Toll Brothers management is hoping to have two or fewer spec homes per community, which "should prevent any inventory buildup." Management is aiming for a mix of 40% affordable luxury homes, 40% move up/luxury and 20% active adult, McCanless says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1128 ET - Toll Brothers is seeing less of a sales slowdown than other homebuilders amid high mortgage rates and affordability concerns because it caters to a wealthier buyer with its average home price over $1 million. As a result, it's not as dependent on mortgage rate buy downs as other companies. But it still needs to offer some incentives to complete sales especially in its affordable luxury segment, which may continue to hurt its margins in the near-term, says Wedbush analyst Jay McCanless. "Toll is still offering design center and other incentives along with selected buy downs to spur traffic," McCanless says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1108 ET - Cracker Barrel has reverted to its old logo after pressure from customers and the White House — but that doesn't answer the question of where its million-dollar marketing strategy will head now, Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks says. The streamlined logo was part of the Southern comfort food restaurant's ongoing strategic transformation. Brooks poses the question of whether Cracker Barrel's choice not to go ahead with the logo change will have other ramifications for that transformation. Whatever it decides, the stakes are high, Brooks says: Cracker Barrel is spending $600 million to $700 million in capital expenditures on the transformation effort over three years. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1054 ET - Restaurant foot traffic appears to have tapered off in August compared with July, Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks says. Benchmark's panel of 31 restaurants had an average year-over-year traffic slow down of 175 basis points in August versus July, Brooks says. This trend is likely setting restaurant companies up for a tough 3Q season. About 40% of the restaurants in Benchmark's panel are on track to miss Wall Street estimates for same-store sales in the current quarter. Meanwhile, 13% of the restaurants are on track to beat with same-store sales by 150 basis points or more. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: OpenAI, Broadcom Make $10 Billion Deal for Custom AI Chips — WSJ
data.description:
By Robbie Whelan and Berber Jin

OpenAI is working with Broadcom to develop custom artificial-intelligence chips, a move that could help alleviate the shortage of powerful processors needed to quickly train and release new versions of ChatGPT.

Broadcom's shares surged nearly 11% in Friday trading after the company announced during an earnings call on Thursday that it had signed up a fourth major AI developer as a customer, and that this new customer had placed a one-time order worth $10 billion for AI server racks using the Palo Alto-based firm's chips.

The new customer is OpenAI, according to people familiar with the matter. The Financial Times reported the arrangement earlier.

Sam Altman, OpenAI's chief executive, has been saying for months that a shortage of graphics processing units, or GPUs, has been slowing his company's progress in releasing new versions of its flagship chatbot.

In February, Altman wrote on X that ChatGPT-4.5, its then-newest large language model, was the closest the company had come to designing an AI model that behaved like a "thoughtful person." But he lamented the delays and high costs that came with developing it.

"It is a giant, expensive model. We really wanted to launch it to plus and pro at the same time, but we've been growing a lot and are out of GPUs," Altman wrote, referring to the model's paid service tiers. "We will add tens of thousands of GPUs next week and roll it out to the plus tier then. (hundreds of thousands coming soon, and i'm pretty sure y'all will use every one we can rack up.)"

In recent years, OpenAI has relied heavily on so-called "off the shelf" GPUs produced by Nvidia, the biggest player in the chip-design space. But as demand from large AI firms looking to train increasingly sophisticated models has surged, chip makers and data-center operators have struggled to keep up.

"If we're talking about hyperscalers and gigantic AI factories, it's very hard to get access to a high number of GPUs," said Nikolay Filichkin, co-founder of Compute Labs, a startup that buys GPUs and offers investors a share in the rental income they produce. "It requires months of lead time and planning with the manufacturers."

To solve this problem, OpenAI has been working with Broadcom for over a year to develop a custom chip for use in model training. Broadcom specializes in what it calls XPUs, a type of semiconductor that is designed with a particular application — such as training ChatGPT — in mind.

OpenAI also recently struck a data-center deal with Oracle that calls for OpenAI to pay more than $30 billion a year to the cloud giant, and signed a smaller contract with Google earlier this year to alleviate computing shortages. It is also embarking on its own data-center construction project, Stargate, though that has gotten off to a slow start.

A person close to OpenAI said the Broadcom chip isn't designed to challenge Nvidia, but rather to plug the gaps in OpenAI's hardware needs.

Broadcom's quarterly earnings beat analyst expectations for revenue and earnings per share. The company forecast that sales would rise 24% year-over-year in the current quarter.

In Thursday's earnings call with analysts, Broadcom CEO Hock Tan said the $10 billion order, expected in next year's summer quarter, would significantly increase the company's 2026 revenue projection.

"The addition of a fourth customer with immediate and fairly substantial demand really changes our thinking of what 2026 would be starting to look like," Tan said.

Once known primarily as a maker of chips for mobile devices, the global AI craze has catapulted Broadcom into the upper echelons of tech companies. Its share price has grown sixfold since the start of 2023 as it moved further into data centers. In August, the company launched its Jericho networking chip, which is designed to help speed up AI computing by connecting data centers as far as 60 miles apart.

By August, Broadcom's market value had surpassed that of oil giant Saudi Aramco, making the chip firm the world's seventh-largest publicly listed company.

Write to Robbie Whelan at robbie.whelan@wsj.com and Berber Jin at berber.jin@wsj.com

data.title: WHO adds GLP-1 drugs for diabetes, others to essential medicines list
data.description:
Key points:
  • WHO stops short of adding Ozempic and Mounjaro to list for obesity alone
  • Cystic fibrosis drug, long-criticised for high price, is included
  • WHO says high prices limit access to medicines

The World Health Organization has added GLP-1 drugs to treat diabetes to its essential medicines list, alongside treatments for cystic fibrosis and cancer, and said it hopes this will improve global access to the costly drugs.

The list, consisting of 523 medicines for adults and 374 for children, is a catalogue of the drugs the WHO believes should be available in all functioning health systems. In the past including a drug, for instance, HIV treatments in the early 2000s, has helped to ensure access for people in poorer countries.

“Rather than letting price be a disqualifying factor, the committee views inclusion in the essential medicines list as a potential catalyst for access,” Dr Lorenzo Moja, head of the WHO secretariat overseeing the list, told Reuters.

The expert committee added the active ingredients in Novo Nordisk’s Ozempic and Eli Lilly’s Mounjaro to the list, to treat type 2 diabetes in conjunction with established cardiovascular disease, chronic kidney disease or obesity.

The drugs were initially developed for diabetes but have also become hugely popular weight-loss drugs, under different brand names. The WHO stopped short of adding them to treat obesity alone, as it also did in 2023.

The committee said this decision provided clear guidance on which patients would most benefit from the therapies.

“High prices of medicines like semaglutide and tirzepatide are limiting access to these medicines,” the WHO statement added, saying that encouraging generic drugmakers to produce the product would also help when patents begin to expire on the drugs next year.

A spokesperson for Novo Nordisk said the company was committed to supporting broader access to its treatments.

Several companies are already working on generic copies of the drugs. Eli Lilly said it too was committed to increasing access, but that off-patent or generic copies of drugs "may be better candidates to meet patient needs and offer cost savings in lower resource settings".

Globally, more than 800 million people worldwide were living with diabetes in 2022, the WHO said. There are also more than 1 billion people with obesity. Earlier this year, a WHO memo said it would recommend the use of the drugs for obesity, a separate step to adding them to the essential list.

The list also includes Vertex Pharmaceuticals’ combination therapy for cystic fibrosis, Trikafta or Kaftrio. Activists have criticised its high price and lack of accessibility for years. The drugmaker declined to comment on the list.

It also includes Merck’s top-selling cancer immunotherapy drug, Keytruda, for the treatment of cervical cancers, colorectal cancers, and non-small cell lung cancers that have spread, or metastasised and it recommended strategies to increase access. Merck said it was committed to reaching patients in need.

The WHO also added rapid-acting insulin analogues, also made by Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly, among others, to the list for treating type 1, type 2 and gestational diabetes.

data.title: Shoppers hunt for vintage Armani after designer's death
data.description:
Online searches for vintage Armani clothes have surged since Italian fashion designer Giorgio Armani died on Thursday, as shoppers scoured second-hand outlets for his styles.

Armani, who led his eponymous company and remained in control of designs right up until his death at 91, was prolific, producing everything from expensive high-end suits to more modestly priced jeans and sportswear under the Emporio Armani brand.

Searches containing the word "Armani" on Vinted, Europe's biggest second-hand clothing marketplace, were almost three times higher than average on Thursday following the news of Armani's passing, a spokesperson for Vinted told Reuters.

U.S. luxury resale site The RealReal said searches for Armani were up 212% on Thursday compared to Wednesday. And Google searches for "vintage Armani" also spiked on Thursday, according to Google Trends data, with interest particularly high in Armani's native Italy and in the UK.

On second-hand fashion app Vestiaire Collective, users across Europe listed their Giorgio Armani pieces for sale on Friday, including a black 1990s silk blazer for 245 pounds ($330.97) and a leather and rabbit fur jacket from 2002 for 571 pounds ($771.36).

Ammar Boulai, who runs luxury second-hand menswear boutique Chez Ammar in Paris, said he would not be surprised to see an uptick in demand for Armani suits from the 1970s and 80s, on the back of the current trend for retro styles with wide trousers and fluid fabrics.

"Four or five years ago, these 80s style suits were impossible to sell. Now they are really in vogue, but impossible to find," said Boulai.

"It's hard to tell how much is in stocks and will re-enter the market. Armani produced a lot, had many sub-brands, so there must be a lot out there... maybe people will open their drawers now," he added.

($1 = 0.7402 pounds)

data.title: Fixed-Income Investors Have Reason to Go Out the Curve — Market Talk
data.description: The Fed looks ready to resume interest-rate cutting and longer-term Treasury yields are falling less than shorter-term ones. Would that be enough to finally trigger the big migration out of money market funds analysts have been predicting since last year? CIBC's Timothy Musial thinks it might. Money market funds have $7.26 trillion deposited, according to the Investment Company Institute. But returns are lower than a year ago, while other fixed income options are paying more. "You do need to move out the curve a little bit to capture the same amount of yield, or even more," he says. "You want to stay in very high quality credit." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: DreamBox Math by Discovery Education Honored With 2025 Tech Edvocate Award
data.description:
CHARLOTTE, NC / ACCESS Newswire / September 5, 2025 / DreamBox Math by Discovery Education was named a winner by the 2025 Tech Edvocate Awards. The 9th annual Tech Edvocate Awards honors the best that edtech has to offer by recognizing outstanding companies, people, and products. Discovery Education is the creator of essential PreK-12 learning solutions used in classrooms around the world.

DreamBox Math by Discovery education was selected as a 2025 Tech Edvocate Award winner in the Best Math or App Tool category. DreamBox Math by Discovery Education is a K-8 online math program that supplements core instruction in the classroom or at home and is independently proven by several third-party research organizations to increase math achievement. DreamBox Math by Discovery Education adapts dynamically to the learner, providing tailored instruction within a highly engaging, gamified learning environment. DreamBox Math lessons are available in both English and Spanish and are standards-aligned.

Along with actionable reporting and tools that empower differentiation for all learners, DreamBox Math offers teachers content-specific professional learning and provides administrators critical insights into student progress. With DreamBox Math, every student is empowered to independently engage in interactive problem solving so they can develop conceptual understanding, apply a variety of strategies, and gain competence and confidence in mathematical reasoning and skills.

In addition, the following were named as finalists:

  • Discovery Education: Best Global EdTech Company

  • DreamBox Reading: Best Literacy App or Tool

  • STEM Careers Coalition: Best STEM/STEAM Education App or Tool

Earlier this year, DreamBox Reading and DreamBox Math were updated with new enhancements that empower teachers to create personalized learning experiences for all learners with inquiry, excitement, and exploration that will hold students' attention while challenging them academically. More information about these enhancements can be found here.

Tech Edvocate Award submissions were evaluated by both a panel of industry professionals and a popular vote. The panel was comprised of two edtech thought leaders, two PreK-12 teachers, one college professor, two K-12 administrators, one college administrator and two PreK-12 parents. Learn more about the Tech Edvocate Awards here.

"It's an honor to be recognized by the Tech Edvocate Awards. Thank you to the panel of judges and to everyone that voted for Discovery Education's solutions," said Brian Shaw, Chief Executive Officer at Discovery Education. "Our team is dedicated to ensuring that all students succeed through engaging and high-quality learning opportunities, and these awards will inspire the Discovery Education team to continue innovating for students and educators."

For more information about Discovery Education's award-winning digital resources and professional learning solutions, visit www.discoveryeducation.com, and stay connected with Discovery Education on social media through LinkedIn, Instagram, TikTok, and Facebook.

###

About Discovery Education

Discovery Education is the worldwide edtech leader whose state-of-the-art, PreK-12, digital solutions help educators engage all students and support higher academic achievement. Through award-winning multimedia content, instructional supports, and innovative classroom tools that are effective, engaging, and easy to use, Discovery Education helps educators deliver powerful learning experiences. Discovery Education serves approximately 4.5 million educators and 45 million students worldwide, and its resources are accessed in over 100 countries and territories. Through partnerships with districts, states, and trusted organizations, Discovery Education empowers teachers with essential edtech solutions that inspire curiosity, build confidence, and accelerate learning. Learn more at www.discoveryeducation.com.

data.title: Both Labor-Market Supply and Demand Are Slowing — WSJ
data.description:
By Paul Kiernan

The jobs report offered fresh signals that both the supply of workers and the demand for employees is slowing-at least partly due to President Trump's border crackdown.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell has said the two-sided slowdown creates a "curious kind of balance" in the labor market.

For starters, economists say unemployment would likely be higher if not for a slowdown in immigration. From January through August, employers added 75,000 nonfarm payrolls per month. That's less than half the 2015-24 average and implies about 1 million fewer jobs being created per year.

At the same time, economists think net immigration-the difference between people entering and leaving the country-is swinging from an estimated 2.7 million people in 2024 to perhaps zero this year. U.S.-born workers are also starting to retire at a faster pace as baby boomers age.

If demand for labor were steady, such a large slowdown in the supply of workers would eventually lead to declining unemployment and accelerating wage growth.

But Friday's data offered little evidence of a broad tightening in the labor market. Unemployment rose to 4.3% from 4.2% and average hourly earnings were up a modest 3.7% from a year earlier.

The unemployment rate among Hispanic workers, some of whom are immigrants, rose to 5.3% in August from 5% in July, a full percentage point higher than for the overall workforce.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: REG - Beazley PLC - Transaction in Own Shares
data.description:
RNS Number : 2953Y Beazley PLC 05 September 2025  

Transactions in own shares

Beazley plc ('Beazley' or the 'Company') announces that on 5 September 2025 it purchased for cancellation through J.P. Morgan Securities plc ('JPMS plc') the following number of its ordinary shares of 5 pence each, pursuant to the authority granted by its shareholders as part of its share repurchase programme, details of which were announced on 5 March 2025.

Ordinary shares

Date of purchase: 5 September 2025           

Number of ordinary shares purchased: 381,400

Highest price paid per share: 789.50p

Lowest price paid per share: 783.50p

Volume weighted average price paid per share: 786.9795p

Since the announcement of the share repurchase programme on 5 March 2025, Beazley has purchased 32,121,200 ordinary shares in aggregate for cancellation. 

In accordance with Article 5(1)(b) of the UK version of Regulation (EU) No. 596/2014 which is part of UK law by virtue of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, the table below contains detailed information of the individual trades made by JPMS plc as part of the share repurchase programme.

data.title: Ethereum bull alert: ETH exchange ‘flux’ turns negative for the first time
data.description:
Key takeaways:

  • Ether’s exchange flux balance turned negative for the first time, signaling aggressive accumulation.

  • ETH price could rally to new highs above $5,000 if key resistance breaks.

Ether is teasing a continuation of its bull cycle as the cumulative exchange net flow turns negative for the first time in history.

Is this the trigger required to push ETH price back into price discovery?

ETH exchange flux balance turns negative 

Founder and CEO of data analytics platform Alphractal, Joao Wedson, highlighted that Ether’s exchange flux balance has turned negative for the first time in history.

The exchange flux balance is a metric that tracks the cumulative net flow of ETH across all exchanges over time and how the flows change over time.

A positive value means more deposits than withdrawals, suggesting potential selling pressure. While a negative balance indicates that more ETH tokens are leaving exchanges than are deposited, it is a sign of accumulation and long-term holding behavior.

“Billions of dollars in ETH are flowing out of exchanges!” Wedson said in an X post on Friday, adding:

“This historic milestone could mark a major shift in ETH investor behavior!”

In other words, ETH is leaving exchanges at an accelerating pace. In particular, exchange outflows have increased since mid-July, reflecting significant accumulation and decreasing supply — both bullish signs.

As Cointelegraph reported, overall exchange ETH balances are at their lowest in 9 years. Ether balance on exchanges is 15.72 million ETH as of Friday, levels last seen in July 2016, per Glassnode data. 

Reducing supply on exchanges means less ETH can be readily sold, potentially leading to a liquidity shortage and higher prices in the long term.

Ether price must reclaim $4,500 to secure recovery

As Cointelegraph continues to report, a key short-term resistance for ETH price remains $4,500, and the bulls need to flip this into new support to go higher.

Note that this level has capped the price since the ETH slipped to $4,300 on Aug. 29.

“$ETH broke out from the falling wedge, retested it, and now hovers above key support,” said crypto trader Jelle in a Friday post on X.

The trader pointed out that a decisive close above $4,500 would leave “very little standing in the way” of a move higher into price discovery.

“$5000 is just the beginning.”

Fellow analyst Donald Dean shared a chart showing ETH price compressing in a tight range within a bull pennant, suggesting that a significant move was imminent.

A daily candlestick close above the upper trendline of the pennant at $4,500 is a must to confirm the breakout.

Based on Fibonacci retracement levels, Dean set the targets for the bull pennant at $5,766, $6,658, and $9,547.

data.title: A lone red rose outside Armani as Milan mourns its 'king' of fashion
data.description:
Key points:
  • Tributes pour in for 'Lord of Fashion'
  • Private funeral to be held on Monday
  • Board member says company will not go 'off-track'

A lone red rose tied to the barrier outside the offices of Giorgio Armani in Milan's fashion district marked a quiet and poignant tribute to the legendary Italian designer on Friday as the city mourned his death at the age of 91.

The "king" of Italian fashion died on Thursday, half a century after he founded his namesake company, which has since grown into a luxury goods conglomerate spanning haute couture to perfume, make-up and even furniture.

"Beyond the artist, beyond the great king of fashion, we've lost a man with a big heart," Milan resident Gabriella Voccia told Reuters. "It's so sad."

In the tree-lined cul-de-sac housing the Armani offices, clothes deliveries arrived as usual while an exhibition area that has hosted catwalk shows was prepared as a chamber to hold Armani's coffin for the public to pay their respects.

There will be a private funeral on Monday afternoon.

"With Armani, an era comes to an end, an era where Italy was able to showcase its creativity in the world," said Milan resident Stefano Del Frate.

The death of Armani, who kept a tight grip over the empire he created, has shorn Italy of a national icon, prompting an outpouring of grief and tributes from celebrities, shoppers and fashion leaders.

"He kept his independence and I think that ... what he would like is continuity," Federico Marchetti, founder of e-retailer Yoox-Net-a-Porter (YNAP) and an Armani Group board member since 2020, told Reuters in an interview.

By holding onto his company in full, Armani set himself apart from the owners of other Italian brands which are now in foreign hands.

"He's definitely a symbol for Italy, no? Because he didn't sell his company," Marchetti said, adding that family members involved in the running of the group and its management would stay true to his legacy thanks to the very detailed guidelines Armani left for the future of the company.

"There's no possibility to go off track too much," said Marchetti who in 2018 sold YNAP to Cartier-owner Richemont .

Tributes to the designer, who started out as a window dresser at La Rinascente department store in Milan's main square before his displays caught the eye of designer Nino Cerruti, flooded the front pages of Italian papers.

"The world's embrace for the Lord of Fashion," Milanese daily Corriere della Sera wrote, next to the picture of a smiling Armani with a pencil in his hand.

"At work until the very end," the newspaper wrote, describing how, until shortly before his death, Armani had been reviewing dresses for a planned September 28 catwalk show.

Armani's show is scheduled to close Milan's womenswear fashion week later this month in the courtyard of Pinacoteca di Brera, the city's main art gallery, an event which was also intended to celebrate the brand's 50-year anniversary.

The designer's impact was global, and across the decades he dressed celebrities from Richard Gere to Julia Roberts to Lady Gaga. Questions now turn to where the company he led goes next and who takes the helm.

"I am shocked," said Angelique Benfield, passing by the Giorgio Armani store in Beverly Hills. "So definitely an icon in the fashion industry."
